sentences.text: Then I went to stay with them.
I was-- it was really-- there was no Jewish committees.
There was no place where I can turn.
I just came back from this hell-- not where am I going-- how am I going-- of course, there is no dead anymore, facing.
Once I came home, I was going some place, to-- Lucia says Mary, there was a-- there was a Russian soldier calling you.
You have to go to the court, because they have to investigate you.
I said, a Russian soldier?
Because the Russians took over already.
Again, again, after they lost the war with the Germans.
I see it and I say, oh my God.
He came back.
He came back, and he was polite kind of.
He says, I don't know nothing.
I have a duty to take you to the court because they have to investigate.
I didn't [INAUDIBLE], and I said, I have to take the child with me?
And he said, I don't think so.
And I went there and they asked me, sit down. and I [INAUDIBLE].
They turn it to something-- [INAUDIBLE].
He says, Mrs.
Stolar, you were-- he took-- that's when I show him the document.
I should have kept it.
Damn it.
I give everything for this sacrament.
It was a symbol of-- and he took it and never gave it back to me, and I told him the story.
Well, but we have a lot of traitors.
They were-- they were spying for Russia.
You were so close to the Germans, and you know the language, and this and that.
And I said, sir, do you know something about Jewish people being under the German regime?
Do you know?
And I told him.
And I was so-- I cried, and I was so emotional, and I told the story.
Anyway, that's it.
Left me.
Oh, not enough.
No, that's it.
Then he said, Mrs-- Mrs-- Mrs.
Stolaskow, Stolar, we are going to give you a soldier who's going to be your companion, and we are going to go through the bazaar-- to the-- the bazaar is a market.
And you are going other places with a lot of people, where it's well-known for-- for crowds.
And we are going to look, and maybe you will recognize somebody who was a traitor for the Germans against us.
Pointing out, because you were here, you were here.
And I said, sir, please.
I have no-- I couldn't.
He said no, you have to do it.
You have to do it.
And I did it for several days.
The soldier was sick of me, and I was sick of him, and they discontinued this thing.
It's so stupid, damn it.
And-- well, anyway.
Now I am-- I left-- Now, this was Russia, Soviet Russia already.
There was Poland and Soviet Russia.
The part of [NON-ENGLISH] belongs to Russia.
I was angry.
I hear people going to Germany, from Germany they go, they receive compensation.
Then they go to Canada.
They go to United States of America.
What-- they go to Palestine, which was still Palestine, not Israel.
You know?
Right.
So where did you go?
Then I say, why the hell did I go-- come to Rovno?
All the people who were caught like I was caught in-- in Poland, they will all travel to America, to Israel.
And I went to this stupid city where all this hell happened to me, to Rovno-- which I didn't find anybody there anyway.
No-- I went back to the-- OK, now I have to wait for-- for-- this is not-- you couldn't just leave Rovno.
You have to go to the-- to the city hall, and ask for-- for permission.
And finally I got my permission and we emigrated to Levice.
This was when they lost the war, the Germans, Poland took over a piece of German, Schlesien.
This is the thing you have to know, geography.
They took out-- they claim it-- 200 years ago, it belonged to Poland, anyway.
They took it over, and then they placed refugees, Polish people there.
And Jewish, too, I mean, automatically.
We want to survive-- they-- many, many-- They started to come back from Russia.
The ones who run away started out in '45, '46.
And I went there.
Rented-- they gave me an apartment.
They-- they gave me an apartment with two-- with a couple of Germans.
They were registered to leave this place.
They have to leave-- they chased them out, the Polacks, evacuated them to deep Germany, to real Germany.
This couple, the name was [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
I must tell you, they were a darling couple.
I must tell you the truth.
They were old, and he was a very-- a city hall worker.
He was retired.
She was so nice.
Would you know, she cleaned up the apartment before they left?
She cleaned, she cleaned, and I said you don't have to do that Mrs.
[NON-ENGLISH].
No, no.
I love to leave you everything so nice.
And she was crazy about the attic.
Elderly people, they have some-- someplace in Potsdam, in Potsdam.
Over there they're going to evacuate.
And they were very-- And I told them the story.
He was-- he was-- she was crying.
He was very-- he said, I wouldn't believe it.
That you wouldn't-- I wouldn't believe my people did those things.
Well, whatever.
I stayed in this Gliwice.
The name of the city was Gliwice.
In German, Gleiwitz.
In Polish-- the Poles named it Gliwice.
I stayed there in this beautiful apartment.
And-- people said in Walbrzych, which is Waldenburg-- there's another city called Walbrzych.
In German, Waldenburg.
This is more Jewish.
More-- many Jewish are there.
It's a different atmosphere.
I travel to Walbrzych.
I left-- I left the apartment.
I went to Walbrzych.
They gave me also another apartment after Germans left it over, the ones that were evacuated.
And this animal, Kola, traveled there, too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was still after me.
This is absolutely unbelievable.
People say, no, no, no, I didn't do the right thing.
I begged, we were hidden from him, drunk, and it came.
And I went to the police finally at once, and they told him never to come over, never, never, never.
But he came after a little while.
He came.
My friend said, you know what you should have done?
Like he was saying-- because I was living the second floor-- I should have pushed with my foot, and being an invalid, I should pushed him, and he would drop dead from over staircase.
But finally, I was rid-- his sister, Lucia, didn't agree with him.
And she said, Mary, believe me, he takes-- he calls me terrible names, blah blah, whatever.
While I was in Walbrzych, I met this-- well, I don't want to.
I was in Walbrzych.
The emigration started to go to Israel.
Yes, when I was in Walbrzych, people from Rovno-- I met some people that said, did you hear about your husband, Mary?
And I said, no, I know that he was killed in the army.
He was caught as a prisoner of war, and being Jewish, they killed him, the Germans.
Oh no, they don't-- they didn't.
Your husband was-- came to Rovno.
Shortly after I left Rovno with my son, he arrived in Rovno-- not shortly, but almost shortly.
I don't know the dates.
He arrived, and they told him, oh, Mary's alive.
She survived with little-- with your little son.
And she took-- And there is a Polish guy, Kola, who took her out.
She heard you are alive.
She probably heard that you are alive.
She run out with Kola.
He was very upset.
And he started out-- he became angry.
He met a woman.
Finally, I don't want him.
Then he met a woman, they told me, a very nice attractive woman-- single--he was a very good-looking man.
They were going to get married.
And he got in an argument with a-- with a Russian officer.
And the Russian regime is not America, you know.
They can go away with murder.
Something happened, he beat him up, and he died.
The Russian officer died.
They arrested my-- my husband, and they're going to-- there was no death penalty already.
It was already a little too late because they cancel a little bit the death penalty at the time.
Not for traitors, just for-- for killing somebody.
He said in self-defense.
He was going never to leave jail.
He was going to get-- they were going to take him out into Siberia-- over there where you stay there forever.
And he-- and she went, this girl who was going to marry him, was very much ashamed, and very much in love, whatever.
She went in the woods, to the forest and she hanged herself.
She hanged herself, and he find out, he hanged himself in jail.
And he came, there it was in the newspaper, and everybody said, you know Chiam Stolar hanged himself?
And this girl, her parents, she was the only child-- his parents-- her parents, God-- and that's it.
Then I met--
When I went to this Walbrzych, when I'm telling you after the everything, I started to work.
I take-- my son started to attend school.
And I met this animal.
I call him animal, I'm sorry.
He's-- he's-- to me, I think he still lives here in Brooklyn.
I met him.
He was married to a shiksa.
He brought her from Russia.
He was the one who survived in Russia.
I told you many Jews run to Russia.
He's one of many who survived in Russia.
We met-- I met him through a friend, it's a coincidence.
And he was in process of divorcing her.
They have a child, and she was-- he says she was anti-Semitic, I don't believe that.
I met him.
And he was glued to me.
I couldn't get rid of him.
But I started to like him, kind of.
Must have been, love is blind.
And I said, look, I don't want nothing to do with you.
You are not divorced, and I know absolutely I've got enough trouble in my life.
And he said, I am in process of divorcing.
And I said, we'll see, I don't know.
You know, Jackie, I've got such good opportunities to remarry, and I didn't.
I must admit I didn't.
That was a mistake.
And this man came to me with papers and said, here.
I said, what happened?
I am divorced.
And I-- and he-- and I married him.
We got civilian marriage.
We're not chuppah, not this-- just in the city hall.
And we emigrated to Israel. 1950 we emigrated to Israel. 1950.
In Israel, she married a Jew-- the Ukrainian wife, a coincidence.
She married a Jew over there after the divorce.
He adopted her child, what was from my husband's child.
And they emigrated to Israel, too.
After that-- after we-- When I came to Israel, I started already to be sorry.
He was woman chaser, and also in terrible shape.
I would never be able to buy a newspaper.
I would hide the money, I would put it in the bank.
And I just couldn't get-- I applied for divorce in Israel.
And in the Jewish law, a woman has nothing to do.
Today's a little bit different, but over there, in the Jewish law, a woman has nothing to say.
This husband doesn't want divorce.
The rabbi said, lady, your husband doesn't want divorce.
We can't divorce you.
I was so damn angry.
I've got cousins here in America, American-born.
It's another hell of a story.
They never-- they were very, very estranged with each other.
When I find out about them many years ago, I was jumping for joy-- I have also somebody, a relative of my own.
And I wrote to them that I would like to come and visit them, and they sent me a paper.
I find them out through the Jewish committee in Poland.
They send me a sponsor.
I did-- they wouldn't buy a ticket for me.
And I went to America.
I met them, 1956.
They all lived in Trenton, New Jersey.
I met them all.
There were several of them.
Those are my father's brother's children.
They emigrate to Israel-- to America many years ago.
I don't know why my father didn't do it.
I do know now.
I was told because there was health reasons.
My father was very bald.
I never knew him for having hair-- and something was an infection, and they wouldn't let him pass through.
This is fate, damn fate.
When those brothers-- OK, those cousins, I went to them in 1956.
I stayed a few months.
I couldn't depend on them too much.
But I could get a visa.
I went back, and I applied for a-- for a green card, and they said I can emigrate with my husband, and my son didn't want to go, and I was a very big mistake to leave my son and go.
But I wanted so much to get rid of him, then I figure I'll get a divorce in America.
When I receive the-- from the American consulate as I am entitled to emigrate, I told him, go, go.
I will follow you.
I have four months time, says the visa.
I was so happy to get rid of him.
He went.
He wouldn't leave me alone.
He kept writing letters.
I--the first opportunity I came out there, I eventually left here, and I went to Mexico for a Mexican divorce, and I divorced him finally, get rid of him.
And I should have gone back to Israel, which I didn't.
It wasn't-- I didn't have the money.
I didn't-- I find a job here.
I started out working.
He married about another three times, four times.
Pig.
And then my son used to come-- my son went to the army.
I went to visit him, and I went visit there, and visit back and forth.
And-- and that's it.
I-- there is another thing, I was going to get married, and I run away from the ceremony, at the last minute.
A nice Jewish, American young man.
That was a very big mistake.
I have pictures.
A very big mistake.
I didn't love him, but he as a very decent, nice-- he was a supervisor in a post office, an American.
And he was 35, and I was 40.
To the last minute.
I was engaged first, buy furniture-- he bought furniture and an apartment.
They started the ceremony with the rabbi-- I ran away.
My life is a hell, Jackie.
It's a hell, it's a hell, it's a hell.
Do I have to tell you about my health?
I worked in an office.
A friend of mine.
A friend of mine said, Mary, there is a woman giving birth to a-- to a baby.
We're going to need somebody in the office.
And I said, me in the office?
My-- my English is still not this, my spelling.
And she said, oh Mary, I know you, you will manage.
And then I went for an interview, and they hired me.
And I worked there 10 long years, doing-- writing distributions.
It was frankly-- a corporation.
They are closed up.
They were sold about 10, 15 years ago.
They closed it up and they sold it.
It was in 1960.
I worked-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- then I emigrated back to-- well, anyway-- then I-- I worked there, and I met this Harry, whatever.
My life was going on.
I was going to work, eight hours a day.
I moved to Flushing in Queens, got a nice apartment.
I used to go to Roseland dancing, because dance  was my-- I wanted to be a dancer.
Anyway, it's a long story why I wanted to be a dancer when I was young.
And I used to go to Roseland dancing, and I was dating a few times, but I'm not the type, and I didn't like the way the-- the men.
And I used to take the subway because I didn't want a ride.
I'm a different mentality, different type of a person.
And I-- and I spent the weekend, library is a very important part of my life.
I can't exist without a library.
Then I read.
Weekends I clean the apartment.
I spoke to friends.
I went to Roseland.
Not every weekend.
And I go to work during the week, work, work, work, traveling to work, till 10 years.
After this I finished, and I decided I'm going to move to Israel, 1971, '72.
I moved to Israel.
My son finished the army.
I find an apartment.
I borrowed this little apartment.
I stayed there, and I looked for a job.
I find a job in a store.
And I said to my son, I'm going to the States, because I'm an American, I have no problem.
And I'm going to work another year or two, because I've got a few thousand dollars saved.
I borrowed this apartment.
I'll come back.
I went back to the States, and I started to work.
And I just started to do different kind of jobs.
And I figure-- well, how about-- and I didn't even have-- I took a furnished room, and eventually I stayed here.
I stayed, and I frankly, didn't go back because it was too late.
And they were going to close it up somehow.
I find that place and I-- First I work with the elderly people as a nurse's aide.
I learned to give the injection.
A very diabetic lady who was a millionaire on Park Avenue-- she had got a housekeeper, and a cleaning woman.
And I was the one that taught me how to put here, give her the insulin every morning.
Then I stayed, she died.
I worked with another one, and then I started to work with FEX-- Federal Employment, FEX, called in short.
And they place elderly people.
Started to receive my social security.
And they place elderly people in jobs in clericals and factories.
And I started to work.
I worked in COJO in office, Consulate of Jewish Organization called.
I translated the Russians, went with them to social offices to be a translator, with the Russian immigrants.
Then they-- they finished the FEX-- out of the business partnership, copartnership took it over.
They called themselves Partnership for the Homeless.
This is an office in Seventh Avenue, located, and they are organized.
They're helping placing elderly people in jobs.
I am with this place, with the partnership from the very beginning, the supervisor Linda Nelson.
They placed me in several organizations.
I worked.
Then I became ill.
I stopped work after the surgery.
In August it's going to be a year I've been back to work.
My surgery was a year and a half ago.
After about eight, nine months, I received chemotherapy.
I found out what my nightmare-- I still don't believe it.
It's absolutely unbelievable, not because I'm untouchable, but because it's the circumstance.
And I stay, I work with them at the-- school volunteers offices.
I work now with the organization-- with the organization.
It's not an organization, it's an office.
This is a-- called-- didn't you hear the school volunteers offices?
They are a very prestigious office, I assume.
They are located in [INAUDIBLE] Park Avenue.
They are-- employed several many retired teachers and volunteers.
They have meetings, and they have done lots of clerical work.
They are very polite.
One of them, you always find the black sheep in the family.
But the supervisors are very nice to me.
And they didn't know nothing about me, very little.
Anyway, maybe the supervisor, a man, is very, very dull.
A young man, Walter.
Before I end it, I was-- when I moved to Israel--
Before I end it, when I moved to Israel in 1972, when I rented-- and I bought a little apartment, I wasn't working.
I looked for something, but then I sit down, and I was reading-- writing my biography.
Everybody was shocked and surprised that I didn't do it so far.
My old biography's in my son's home, edited, but he was-- he was the one who was supposed to translate it into English, because I wrote it in Polish, which is my native language.
He never did it.
Put us in-- I'll show you my son's magazines here.
He put-- he put two articles, and I wrote the articles in Hebrew.
My Hebrew reading, I can-- Yiddish is-- I'm fluent in Yiddish writing and reading.
In Hebrew I'm a little worse.
Then-- according what I-- I didn't like it.
He polished it up a little bit.
And I didn't like it.
Boy, did I didn't like it.
I was very angry.
And I said, Eric, first of all, you should ask my permission.
How dare you say you polish it up a little bit?
This is hell.
He said, mother, he says literature, he says this and this.
And he was sorry later. and I didn't-- and I said, I want the original, this translation.
He said, he's still going to translate it.
Everybody who ever knows about me is very shocked.
I saw books-- I don't read it anymore-- I saw books the survivors was written.
That's night and day.
Now, talking about the Holocaust again, ladies.
Unfortunately, I know a few of them.
And they were-- they didn't even see a German soldier in the eyes.
Oh look, a survivor-- a Holocaust survivor.
The only time they saw a uniform, they saw it maybe in the television.
I don't want to squeal on them.
I don't want to say they applied for compensation or whatever.
Let them.
I don't care.
But this is-- And then the books what I read, which is true, of course.
Because nobody's going to write a biography if it's not true.
They can't apply for a form, and lie in the form to the Germany.
And I've been to Germany twice, or three times I went.
Then once, when I went to Germany in 1972 from Israel, I looked in the telephone book.
I figure I'm going to find out if this German is alive, Franz Kanola.
And I find in the telephone book, don't you think I'm find him?
It was unbelievable.
And I called up, and my German was not so polished anymore.
I spoke to somebody from Franz Kanola family.
I said, may I speak to Mr.
Franz Kanola?
And he said, [GERMAN].
He said, no, he's--I'll give you the number where to reach him.
I reached him.
And I said, Franz, do you speak English?
And he said no.
[GERMAN] Then he said, then I'm going to tell, because my-- my German is mixed with English now with Jewish.
He said, why?
Who is it?
And I said, it's Maria.
You remember Maria?
Because he was really in love with me.
He was crazy about Eric.
This little blonde boy.
Oh, my God.
Who's-- where am I, this and then.
And we met.
Then I said, Franz, I'm going to tell you something, but you've got to sit down, not to faint.
You know all those affairs, and all this work, and all this knowledge, and all those things, and all those conversations, do you know who I am?
[GERMAN].
[GERMAN]-- NOT you.
Yes, sir.
[GERMAN], Franz.
He gave me a little note.
He wrote down, we went to a cafeteria.
We went to a cafeteria.
We got some coffee, and he wrote down a note by hand, not typed.
He knows me from this and that, this and that.
And I never received any compensation.
The irony-- people don't believe it.
A friend of mine said, oh Mary, you probably received a bundle.
If these people didn't-- did receive or they didn't-- they weren't there.
And I said I don't receive a dollar.
Not myself, and not my son.
It's disappointments in my life is going on.
And I went, I took a lawyer, but anyway-- now they have something.
I knew things.
They're giving the Jews several million dollars, a few million dollars.
They should give it out to people who are financially-- I mean, the ones, the survivors, naturally.
On 22nd Street, just for fun I went there.
And I spoke a bit of interview a lady, and I told her, and she said, every word what you said, I believe every word.
I know who survived.
I know you.
Every single word, because I was there.
And she's the-- But Mrs.
Stolar, it's too late.
In August they're starting out something, whatever is financially.
And I said to a friend of mine, I said, Betty, I know it's not justice.
It's-- it's ironic, nobody believes it, but I don't care anymore since the operation.
Just don't care.
Money is absolutely not any more important to me.
I will survive, and I just want to have a few years to stick around to see my grandchildren.
Then my son was here in July.
He came here, exactly a month-- a year ago.
He lectures at a university.
My son, after all the hell was he was through, and then it's not the end of it.
The real hell my son was through was 11 and 1/2 years ago, with his health.
My son is a very big athlete, 6'2'.
He look like a Greek God.
You see the pictures, right?
He's so highly intellectual-- he speaks several languages, he translates a lot of translations.
He's the editor of the highly intellectual magazine, which here I'll show you in a minute, the magazine.
It's here.
I put it together in this bag, because I have to give it away.
A few to the consulate, a few to the library, I promised.
Now, he was in his trophies for swimming, trophies for throwing the javelin, trophies for swimming.
He never smoked or drank.
Lectured.
Worked in the governments and the party and everything.
Never-- what happened to him 11 and a 1/2 years ago?
His health.
His prostate was removed.
He wouldn't tell me-- a year and a half, he wouldn't tell me.
Just wouldn't tell me.
He said, mother, something happened to me.
A year and a half after this, he told me, which is 10 years ago.
Altogether 11 and 1/2 years.
He's very unusual.
He takes pain very easy.
Doesn't like pity.
He doesn't like to speak about the-- about this Holocaust.
He also filled out the forms because everybody did.
And I said, do it.
At least-- oh, mama, I don't want nothing from them.
I want nothing to do with this, please.
And now he has these two children.
Thank God, he's crazy about them, and they are crazy about him.
He lives for them.
He's built a house in settlement near Tel Aviv-- I don't know if you know, [NON-ENGLISH].
It's called [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know, you heard about this?
Those are settlements.
He built a beautiful house.
He lives there, he lectures at the university twice a week.
The radio, and this, very active and very, very busy.
Too busy.
Doesn't take care of his health.
The-- swim-- the swimming is to him terribly important.
He swims twice a day for-- for an hour, each day.
He said, it makes him absolutely great, feel great.
And then he keep telling me, mama, you're going to be all right.
He can be a little sarcastic sometimes.
And he kills himself for this country of his.
The Sabras were born there-- they ran away to America and make money.
He was, he was.
He's a paratrooper in the-- in the air force.
He's a lieutenant paratrooper, and he did so much for this country, and the country didn't do nothing for him.
And I just want to say the young Israel is bulky, healthy guys.
They avoid the army, and they come to United States to make money, and they are born even in Israel.
That's it.
What can I tell you?
What else should I say?
I am miserable, extremely miserable.
This is the first time I am so depressed.
I wasn't depressed in the Holocaust so much.
End of my life, it doesn't give me absolutely any pleasure.
And-- and I am depressed, and that's all.
I'm terrified.
You're welcome.
Then tell me, how is this going to work?
